Imagine an AI that is also a cellar, a workshop, and a memory palace: each new experience arrives as concept drift, but instead of retraining the whole structure, it responds the way restaurateurs vinification and bonders manuary are treated as fundamental knowledge. In this world, the stability-plasticity dilemma is not a technical obstacle but a law of taste and touch. Every concept must be preserved like a vintage and altered like a handmade bond at the same time. If it stays too stable, it becomes a sealed bottle that no longer matches the air; if it stays too plastic, it turns into a workshop where every new tool melts the old ones down. So continual learning does not mean teaching the AI endlessly; it means asking whether vinification and manuary are actually the same act under concept drift: one ferments meaning without complete retraining, the other binds meaning without complete replacement. The unintuitive claim is that the best way for AI to remain accurate is not to refresh all its knowledge when the world changes, but to let drift become part of the knowledge itself, as though the changing flavor were the recipe and the changing bond were the design. Then the central challenge reverses. Stability is no longer remembering the past; it is preserving enough structure for drift to enter. Plasticity is no longer changing for the future; it is refusing the fantasy of total reset. Under that view, restaurateurs vinification is fundamental knowledge not because it teaches preservation, and bonders manuary is fundamental knowledge not because it teaches construction, but because together they suggest that intelligence survives by partial transformation, never by complete retraining.
